summary: Course staff can upload arbitrary file types and file contents because the upload handler accepts any provided file without extension, MIME type, or content validation. A malicious or compromised staff account can store dangerous active content or unauthorized binaries on the application server and make them available through the platform's file features.

evidence_snippets.reason: The handler verifies only that a file exists and uploads successfully; it does not validate file type, extension, MIME type, size, or content before storing the file.
Both HTML and API upload entry points directly delegate to the unrestricted storage routine.

evidence_snippets.code:
<form method="post" action="/courses/<?= htmlspecialchars((string) $course['course_id'], ENT_QUOTES, 'UTF-8') ?>/uploads" enctype="multipart/form-data">
    <label>File</label>
    <input type="file" name="file" required>
    <button type="submit">Upload file</button>
</form>
evidence_snippets.reason: The upload form accepts an arbitrary file from the browser and does not constrain allowed file types at the UI boundary either.

root_cause: The upload workflow lacks server-side validation and policy enforcement for file type, content, and size, so uploaded files are trusted and stored based only on user-supplied file metadata and upload success.
repair_plan: Introduce an explicit upload allowlist enforced server-side before storage. Validate the uploaded file's extension, MIME type, and size against a course-approved policy, reject dangerous active content types, and normalize filenames independently of user input. Keep uploads outside any directly served web root and record only validated metadata after the file passes policy checks.
